attributes.stem: A 45-year-old woman is admitted to hospital with left leg swelling and pain three days after total hip replacement surgery. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.9°C. Her left leg is swollen from mid-thigh to ankle with tenderness in the calf. Ultrasound confirms an extensive deep vein thrombosis extending from the popliteal to the common femoral vein.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following tests should be performed before commencing anticoagulation?
options: Protein C and protein S levels
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with an extensive deep vein thrombosis (DVT) following hip surgery. Before starting anticoagulation, it is essential to obtain baseline blood tests to ensure safe administration of therapy. Renal function testing is important to determine suitability for low molecular weight heparins and direct oral anticoagulants (e.g., apixaban, rivaroxaban).

D-dimer testing is not necessary in this case as the diagnosis has already been confirmed by ultrasound. D-dimer is most useful as a rule-out test when the pre-test probability of DVT is low to moderate.

CT pulmonary angiogram would only be indicated if there were clinical features suggesting pulmonary embolism (PE) such as chest pain, shortness of breath, or hypoxia. This patient has no such symptoms and has a confirmed DVT as the cause of her presentation.

As per recommendations from Choosing Wisely Australia, testing for inherited thrombophilia (e.g., antithrombin deficiency, protein C deficiency, protein S deficiency, Factor V Leiden mutation, prothrombin gene mutation) should not be performed in venous thromboembolism associated with a transient risk factor (e.g., immobility, trauma, and surgery). The presence of a clear provoking factor (recent surgery) makes inherited thrombophilia an unlikely primary cause of this thrombotic event.

After confirming renal function is adequate, anticoagulation can be initiated with either low molecular weight heparin or a direct oral anticoagulant. The choice of agent may also depend on other factors such as compliance, cost, and patient preference.
learningPoints: Renal function testing is required to determine suitability for anticoagulation with low-molecular weight heparin and direct-acting oral anticoagulants.
Testing for inherited thrombophilia should not be performed in venous thromboembolism associated with a transient risk factor (e.g., immobility, trauma, and surgery).
references: Choosing wisely [Internet]. [cited 2024 Oct 25]. Recommendations from the Haematology Society of Australia and New Zealand. Available from: https://www.choosingwisely.org.au/recommendations/hsanz4

Tran HA, Gibbs H, Merriman E, et al. New guidelines from the Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand for the diagnosis and management of venous thromboembolism [published correction appears in Med J Aust. 2019 Jul;211(2):94. doi: 10.5694/mja2.50260] [published correction appears in Med J Aust. 2020 Feb;212(3):108. doi: 10.5694/mja2.50486]. Med J Aust. 2019;210(5):227-235. doi:10.5694/mja2.50004
